coredata.dc:title: One-pot microbial approach to synthesize carbon dots from baker's yeast-derived compounds for the preparation of antimicrobial membrane
coredata.dc:creator: Ghorbani, Mahdi
Tajik, Hossein
Moradi, Mehran
Molaei, Rahim
Alizadeh, Arash
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized hydrothermally from secreted metabolites (so-called postbiotics) of baker's yeast. The CDs were verified and characterized by particle size analyzer, transmission electronic microscopy, UV–visible/fluorescence, Fourier-transform infrared, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic. Homogeneous N and P-doped CDs (∼4.1 nm) with abundant functional groups showed a dose-dependent antimicrobial activity on Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and fungal strains. A concentration-dependent antioxidant power based on reducing the power of iron in the presence of CDs was reported. CDs were found to be non-toxic at concentrations below 3.5 mg/mL in the human colon cancer cell line (HCT-116) and TM4 cells (mouse Sertoli cell line). Moreover, CDs were used for the fabrication of antimicrobial bacterial nanocellulose membranes. Biocellulose-CDs membrane depicted broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity on nine selected bacterial and fungal strains. Taken together, the synthesized CDs and biocellulose membrane could be considered as potential antimicrobial/antioxidant additive and antimicrobial packaging material, respectively.

head.ce:title: One-pot microbial approach to synthesize carbon dots from baker's yeast-derived compounds for the preparation of antimicrobial membrane

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized hydrothermally from secreted metabolites (so-called postbiotics) of baker's yeast. The CDs were verified and characterized by particle size analyzer, transmission electronic microscopy, UV–visible/fluorescence, Fourier-transform infrared, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic. Homogeneous N and P-doped CDs (∼4.1 nm) with abundant functional groups showed a dose-dependent antimicrobial activity on Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and fungal strains. A concentration-dependent antioxidant power based on reducing the power of iron in the presence of CDs was reported. CDs were found to be non-toxic at concentrations below 3.5 mg/mL in the human colon cancer cell line (HCT-116) and TM4 cells (mouse Sertoli cell line). Moreover, CDs were used for the fabrication of antimicrobial bacterial nanocellulose membranes. Biocellulose-CDs membrane depicted broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity on nine selected bacterial and fungal strains. Taken together, the synthesized CDs and biocellulose membrane could be considered as potential antimicrobial/antioxidant additive and antimicrobial packaging material, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an attractive member of zero-dimensional nanomaterials with special physicochemical features such as significant luminescence, photostability, low toxicity, facile one-pot bottom-up synthesis, biocompatibility, antimicrobial performance, antioxidant activity, and UV blocker [1–6]. Depending on the size, quantity, composition, and application, various techniques have been identified for synthesizing CDs including arc discharge, laser ablation, pyrolysis, heating, and hydrothermal techniques [7]. However, the hydrothermal method is one of the most popular techniques due to its low costs, easy implementation, and environmental compatibility [2]. The precursors can be some bulk carbon sources (top-down approach) or small molecule precursors that have been carbonized by decomposition/fusion/carbonization procedures (bottom-up approach) [8]. In a hydrothermal bottom-up method, small organic molecules are fused at relatively high temperatures, and CDs below 10 nm are generated [9,10]. Regardless of the synthesis method, the choice of the starting precursors is determined by the expected features of the synthesized CDs such as size, morphology, surface charge, hydrophobicity, toxicity, and photoluminescence characteristics [1]. Many efforts are being made to utilize different sources such as biowaste and pure chemicals [11,12], bioactive molecules [13], and waste biomass [14] as CDs precursors.

The microbial approach has generated an attractive interest as bio-precursors for CDs synthesis due to its low cost, low toxicity, availability, and abundance. In this strategy, microbial cells or their extracellular/intracellular metabolites serve as a carbon source for CDs production [15,16]. The microbial cell is a rich source of organic molecules such as carbohydrates, peptidoglycan, proteins, and peptides, which contain such heteroatoms (generally O, N, S, and P), therefore creates an excellent functionalized surface for biomass-derived CDs without any additional modification (i.e., doping). As an example, CDs have already been synthesized by microbial cells of Lactobacillus plantarum [16] , Staphylococcus aureus [1], and Escherichia coli [17]. As an alternative to microbial cells, different extracellular metabolites (so-called postbiotics) which are produced by microorganisms during commercial fermentation [18], can be used as a starting material for CDs synthesis. Postbiotics consist of both bioactive organic metabolites produced by microbial cells and substances present in the culture media which may be used as sources of carbon for water soluble CDs production, although hydrophobic CDs have also been synthesized from other sources [19].

Antimicrobial packaging is a type of active packaging that inhibits or delays the growth of food spoilage/ pathogenic microorganisms [20]. Antimicrobial packaging systems can modify the microbiota of food and improve its shelf life. A variety of polymers, antimicrobials, and fillers have been used to prepare antimicrobial packages [21]. Bacterial nanocellulose (BNC or biocellulose) is an extracellular product of the early metabolism of certain bacterial strains, mainly Komagataeibacter spp. Non-parallel nanofabric morphology, high mechanical strength, and high water holding capacity make BNC a suitable medium for different guest molecules, e.g., antimicrobials [22]. Antimicrobials of various origins such as microorganisms, animals, plants as well as nanomaterials have been used in the production of antimicrobial BNC membranes. Recently, the antimicrobial performance of the CDs on different Gram-positive and Gram-negative microorganisms has attracted a great deal of attention [23]. BNC has been used as a polymer carrier for CDs that have been synthesized from white mulberry (Morus alba L.) and postbiotics of Lactobacillus plantarum [10] in order to prepare a high UV- protective and potent antimicrobial membrane [24].

Saccharomyces cerevisiae, also known as baker's or brewer's yeast, is a commercially available probiotic. Yu et al. [15] used S. cerevisiae cells to fabricate CDs with the ability to sense pH and vitamin B12. But there is no work on the use of yeast postbiotics for CDs synthesis. It seems that protein, vitamins, nucleic acids, and glutathione of S. cerevisiae postbiotics can not only be used as a primary carbon source for CDs, however, CDs with N, S, or P heteroatoms are also expected to be produced using postbiotics as a starting precursor. Recently, postbiotics of prokaryote cells (i.e., L. plantarum) was used for CDs synthesis [10], but in the current study, postbiotics of S. cerevisiae (a eukaryote) was used as a precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. The as-prepared CDs were fully characterized and antibacterial, antifungal, antioxidant, and cytotoxic activities were examined. In addition, antimicrobial BNC-CDs membrane was developed through the ex-situ method, and its antimicrobial, microscopic, and spectroscopic properties were also examined.

The Komagataeibacter xylinus subsp. sucrofermentants (BPR 2001), Listeria monocytogenes (PTCC 1298), E. coli (PTCC 1399), S. aureus (PTCC 1112), Salmonella Typhimurium (PTCC 1709), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PTCC 1430), Aspergillus flavus (PTCC 5006), Rhodotorula rubra (PTCC 5255), Aspergillus fumigatus (PTCC 5009), Fusarium solani (PTCC 5284) and S. cerevisiae (PTCC 5269) were obtained from Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST), Tehran, Iran. The tryptic soy (TS) broth/agar (Quelab, Canada), Mueller Hinton agar (Ibresco company, Tehran, Iran), sabouraud dextrose broth (Quelab, Canada), the agar-agar powder (Mirmia, Tehran, Iran), gluten (Faravari fructose NAB company, Tehran, Iran) and molasses (Miandoab Sugar Factory, Miandoab, Iran) were also used. The PTFE sterile syringe filters were obtained from Millipore Inc. (M.A., the U.S.), 2,4,6-Tripyridyl-S-triazine (TPTZ) from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany), ethanol from Pars Alcohol (Shiraz, Iran), and other chemicals and reagents were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA).

Initially, S. cerevisiae was grown in a medium with 2% yeast extract, 1% glucose, and 1% K2HPO4 (pH at 5.8) for 72 h at 27.4 ± 1 °C under aerobic conditions [25]. Then, the obtained fungal suspension was centrifuged (4000 rpm, 10 min) and the supernatant without cells (i.e., postbiotics) was collected aseptically. For CDs hydrothermal synthesis, the as-prepared postbiotics were poured in a Teflon lined hydrothermal autoclave (200 mL) and heated for 7 h at 200 °C. After cooling, the solution was centrifuged (15,000 rpm for 30 min) and filtered using a sterile polytetrafluoroethylene syringe filter (0.22 µm). The filtrate was subsequently freeze-dried and kept in the refrigerator for further use.

The BNC membrane ( Fig. 1) was prepared in a static condition at 30 °C for 3 days, according to the method proposed by Rodrigues et al. [26]. The ex-situ method was applied to produce the antimicrobial BNC membranes (Fig. S1). For this purpose, the BNC membrane was cut and then sterilized with UV light for 2 min under a safety cabinet. The pieces of BNC were then immersed in a selected solution of CDs for 24 h with a loading capacity of 22.33 ± 1.96 mg/cm3. The appearance of BNC with and without CDs were illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and BNC-CDs membrane
Morphology, size, and spectral properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characterization of the CDs was carried out using nanodrop (2000 C, Waltham, USA) in the range of 200–700 nm at 1 nm resolution and spectrofluorometric spectra were obtained with an FP-6500 Jasco fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation increments of 1 nm. The shape and size of the CDs were verified with the transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Leo 906 E, Carl Zeiss, Germany). The size distribution and zeta potential of the CDs was examined using the particle size analyzer (PSA) (Scatteroscope, Qudix Inc., South Korea) and the Laser diffraction particle analyzer (Nanotrac wave II; Microtrac, Krefeld, Germany) at 23 °C, respectively. To investigate the elemental composition of CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Κ-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, 8025-BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system, Berlin, Germany).

The DPPH method was used to assess the antioxidant activity of the CDs [27]. For this purpose, specific volumes of the DPPH solution (4 mg/100 mL methanol) were added to a range of aqueous CDs solutions of different concentrations. Samples were then incubated at ambient temperature for 1 h in darkness, and the absorption of each sample was recorded at 517 nm. Finally, the percentage inhibition of free radicals (%RS) was calculated using Eq. (1). The control antioxidant was butylated hydroxyl toluene (BHT).

where Ablank and Asample represent the absorption rate of the DPPH solution (control) and the CDs + DPPH solution, respectively. IC50 was determined from the plot of %RS vs concentration.

The total antioxidant capacity (TAC) of CDs was also measured by ferric reducing/antioxidant power (FRAP) assay [28]. Concisely, 80 µL of working FRAP reagent [acetate buffer (0.3 M pH 3.6) with a 10 mM solution of TPTZ in 40 mM HCl, and a 20 mM solution of FeCl36 H2O, in a ratio of 10:1:1] mixed with 20 µL of the sample, and incubated in the dark for 30 min at 23 ± 1 °C. The absorbance was measured with a spectrophotometer at 593 nm. The results were expressed as equivalents of ascorbic acid (mg/g DW).

The cytotoxicity of different concentrations of CDs on the human colon cancer cell line (HCT-116) and TM4 cells (mouse Sertoli cell line) was evaluated using the standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-tetrazolium bromide) colorimetric assay [29]. Cells were seeded in 96 well plate and grown at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 95% air and 5% CO2. After reaching the appropriate confluency, the cells were exposed to different concentrations of CDs (0.21, 0.43, 0.87, 1.75, 3.50, and 7 mg/mL) for 24 h and 4 h before the end of the experiment 20 µL of MTT (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) solution (3 mg/mL PBS) was added to each well according to manufacturer’s instruction. After the mentioned period, the cell supernatant was replaced by Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as the substance for lysing the cells. The ELISA reader (Chromate 4300 Microplate Reader) was used for the measurement of absorbance according to the standard protocol. The total oxidant status (TOS) of TM4 cells in the presence of different concentrations of CDs was assessed using a ready-to-use Natos Kit (Navand Salamat Co., Urmia, Iran), which is based on the oxidation reaction occurring through amplification of the molecules present in the reaction medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR NMR, and XRD analysis of CDs and BNC-CDs membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structural properties and surface groups of the CDs and BNC-CDs membrane were investigated at the wavelength of 4000–450 cm−1 using the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet instrument, Nexus® 670, USA). 13C and 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) studies were conducted using resonance frequencies of 250 MHz on Bruker Advance spectrometer (Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were done using Simens D5000 (Siemens AG, Karlsruhe, Germany) X-ray diffractometer with Cu K radiation (λ = 1.54 A˚, 40 kV, 30 mA) on CDs, BNC and BNC-CDs membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of BNC-CDs membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CDs on the morphology and the porous structure of the BNC was investigated with the Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) (SIGMA VP-500; Zeiss, Germany). Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) of CDs investigated with the FESEM (TSCAN, Czech). The mechanical properties of preconditioned (MgNO3 solution; 53 ± 2% RH; at 25 ± 1 °C for 48 h) BNC and BNC-CDs membrane such as strain to break (STB) (%) ratio, the ultimate tensile strength (UTS) (MPa) and Young's modulus (YM) were investigated using a universal testing instrument (STM1, Santam, Tehran, Iran).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of CDs and BNC-CDs membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial properties of the CDs on different bacterial (L. monocytogenes E. coli, S. aureus, S. Typhimurium and P. aeruginosa) and fungal (A. flavus, R. rubra, A. fumigatus, and F. solani) strains were investigated using the well diffusion method [30]. For susceptibility assay, an inoculum of 6 log10 CFU/mL of selected bacterial or fungal strains was spread on the Mueller Hinton and sabouraud dextrose agar using a sterilized swab, respectively. Then, wells with a diameter of 8 mm were created on agar under sterile conditions, and 100 µL of different CDs concentrations (25, 50, and 100 mg/mL) were added to each well. Following 24 h incubation at 37 °C (for bacterial strains) and 72 h incubation at 25 °C (for fungal strains), the size of zone inhibition (ZOI) was measured.

In addition, the disk-covering test was used to investigate the antimicrobial activity of the BNC-CDs membrane [20]. Initially, agars were inoculated in a similar way to well-diffusion by bacterial and fungal strains. Next, a sterilized CD-BNC membrane (8 mm in diameter) was placed on a plate in triplicate. Similar incubation conditions to well-diffusion were also adopted for this trial.

All tests were conducted with three replicates, all data were analyzed by analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Duncan's multiple range test using Graph Pad Prism version 8.01 (GraphPad Software, USA). The statistical significance differences of each data were checked at a significance level of 5% (p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, size, and spectral properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal mechanism of C-dots formation has been divided into five steps, including hydrolysis (hydrolyzation of bacterial cells and producing small molecules such as amino acids, N-acetylmuramic acid, and N-acetylglucosamine acid), condensation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation [31]. The type of precursor and synthesis conditions can affect the degree of carbonization processes and the formation of differently sized sp2-conjugated domains within the CDs, which eventually leads to a different color of the products with emissions ranging from blue to red [1]. Proteins, vitamins, nucleic acids, glutathione, and other compounds that are produced during the yeast growth along with compounds that come from culture medium can be convenient sources for the CDs synthesis since they can be easily polymerized and carbonized [32]. After synthesis, a bright green fluorescence appearance of CDs under UV light was verified and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs was evaluated ( Fig. 2 A and B). As shown in Fig. 1, the UV–Vis spectrum of the CDs reveals a relatively sharp peak (λmax) at 285 nm with a tail extending (quasi-peak) at around 330 nm, which could be attributed to the electronic transition of the π-π * in the conjugated CC groups and n−π* of the CO and −NH2 groups of CD [33]. It confirms that the structure of the CDs consists of aromatic sp2 domains and doped heteroatoms. On the other hand, the steady-state fluorescence emission study of CDs revealed dual emission bands at 354 and 426 nm when the excitation wavelength was located at 290 nm (Fig. 2 B). Although explaining the carbon fluorescence mechanism is always controversial, most researchers have linked PL emissions from CDs to surface electronic states induced by surface functional groups, especially those containing nitrogen and oxygen [34,35]. In addition, it is worth noting that with tuning the excitation wavelength in the range of 300–450 nm, the position of the emission peak and even the intensity remains unchanged. This excitation independent behavior of the emission peak could be corresponding to the single transition mode for big size CDs emission or carboxylic and amide groups linked to the sp2 C domain [36]. The particle size distribution obtained from PSA (Fig. 2 D) confirmed CDs with narrow size distribution and over 99% of the particles were smaller than 20 nm with an average size of 4.1 nm. Furthermore, the TEM micrograph (Fig. 2 C) reveals highly homogeneous CDs without aggregation with a size less than 10 nm. To assess the surface charge of the CDs, the zeta potential was measured. The zeta potential of the CDs was − 47.6 mV, highlighting the presence of negatively charged groups on the surface of CDs in parallel with the FTIR results.

To investigate the elemental composition and surface functional state of hydrothermal carbonization of the postbiotics, XPS analysis of CDs was used ( Fig. 3). The results showed characteristic peaks at 531.73 eV (O 1s), 399.01 (N 1s), 283.36 eV (C 1s), 188.05 eV (P 2s) and 132.32 eV (P 2p), respectively [37]. According to the survey resolution scan of the CDs, as displayed in Fig. S3, the C1s spectrum could be divided into four main peaks which are ascribed to sp3 C (283.1 eV), C-C/CC (283.7 eV), C-N/C-O (284.7 eV), CO (286.3 eV) functional groups, respectively [38]. The three fitted peaks at 531.3, 535.7, and 536.5 eV in O1s spectrum can be attributed to CO, C-O, and C−OH/C−O−C groups, respectively. The N1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.7, 400.2, and 403.6 eV which are corresponds to CC-N, N-C, and N-H nitrogen species, respectively [39]. Also, the two subpeaks fitted in the P2p spectrum can be assigned to P-O (132.2 eV) and P-C/PO (132.7 eV) groups [40].

The NMR analysis can provide important information on carbon numbers in the structure and types of chemical environments, confirming CDs synthesis. In terms of NMR 13C spectrum intensity ( Fig. 4), three major regions between 20 and 60 ppm, around 70 ppm, and between 170 and 190 ppm can be assessed. The first region (20–60 ppm) can be attributed to the sp3 carbon (aliphatic unsaturated -C-C-), and the signals around 70 ppm, maybe due to a carbon in the vicinity of an electronegative element or a carbon bound to an electronegative element [41]. Based on the FTIR results, the presence of oxygen in the structures such as C-O-H and/or OC-C may be taken into consideration. It should be noted that the shoulder near 45 ppm provided proof of nitrogen-bound carbon in a distinct chemical environment [42]. In addition, resonances between 170 and190 ppm demonstrated the presence of CO groups in the carboxylic and aldehyde groups [43]. Similarly, the 1H NMR spectrum of CDs has several signals (Fig. 4), which probably corresponded to the existence of various chemical structures. More specifically, representative signals founded around 1–3 ppm can be attributed to sp3 C–H protons, while peaks between 3 and 6 ppm can be assigned to the protons bonded with hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups. The peaks in the 7–8 ppm region demonstrate aromatic or sp2 protons, and the weak signal between 8 and 9 ppm matches aldehydic protons [41]. However, the chemical composition of CDs was verified by EDX. As shown in Fig. S2, the signal of EDX spot analysis indicates that the CDs consist of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and phosphorous (P) with the expected values of the elemental composition of 28.1%, 2.5%, 18.4%, and 4.6%, respectively.

The potential of a nanomaterial to inhibit free radicals is determined by the DPPH test and measuring the absorption at 517 nm [44]. After adding different CDs concentrations to the DPPH solution (which was dark purple), the color of the solutions faded within an hour (Fig. S4). It is clear that the discoloration may occur due to the presence of a large number of groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl on the surface of CDs in the electron donor section [45]. The IC50, which indicates the removal of 50% of the primary free radicals, was used to demonstrate the antioxidant potential of CDs (Fig. S5A). A high IC50 (0.57 mg/mL) is indicative of a weaker antioxidant activity of CDs synthesized from postbiotics of S. cerevisiae. This fact may be linked to the lower interaction of CDs and free radicals in working solutions or the production of additional OH- radicals [46]. The resulting IC50 is considerably higher than that of BHT (IC50 3.8 μg/mL) and ascorbic acid (IC50 4.34 μg/mL) [27].

The FRAP measures the antioxidant potential in samples through the reduction of Fe3+-TPTZ complex to ferrous iron (Fe2+) by antioxidants present in the sample that produces an intense blue color. The intensity of color and absorption is directly related to the antioxidant properties of the solution. The FRAP assay cannot measure the radical scavenging power [47], then by combining FRAP and DPPH, it is possible to get important Supplementary Information on the antioxidant mechanism of CDs. The FRAP results (Fig. S5B) demonstrated that with increase in the concentration of CDs from 0.001 to 2 mg/mL, the antioxidant property also increases, indicating that the antioxidant activity of CDs may be related to the electron transfer reaction mechanism in which CDs act as a reducing agent by donating a hydrogen atom to the ferric complex. The factionalized groups of CDs (e.g., hydroxyl and carboxylic groups) may act as a reducing agent resulting in a greater reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. CDs at 2 mg/mL represented a TAC of 56 nM of Fe2SO4/L. It seems that the antioxidant properties of current CDs are more due to the electron transfer reaction mechanism than the scavenging performance of CDs.

Assessment of toxicity and risk analysis is of major importance for different substances in association with environment, human and animal well-being. Various factors could play a role in the toxicity of CDs, including the CDs shape, size, charge and surface groups, type of starting precursor, CDs concentration, and type of cell line which is exposed to CDs [48,49]. Therefore, the evaluation of different toxicity aspects is difficult to be generalized. It is believed that multiple mechanisms such as adsorption/accumulation of CDs on the surface of the cell, ingestion/uptake by cells, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation have an impact on toxicity induced by CDs [50]. In order to investigate the toxicity of CDs in the current study, the effect of different concentrations of CDs on the viability of HCT-116 and TM4 cell lines was measured after 24-hour exposure. As illustrated in Fig. 5, there were no cytotoxic effects on HCT-116 and TM4 cell lines up to 1.75 mg/mL of CDs, indicating that the cytotoxic potential of CDs is quite low which could be considered as the potentially non-toxic nanomaterial [49]. Furthermore, the microscopic pictures showed morphological changes in the cells (Fig. 5). Cell destruction and lysis were fairly evident at elevated concentrations (i.e., 7 mg/mL in HCT-116 cells; 3.5 and 7 mg/mL in TM4 cells). No significant differences in cytotoxic effects of different CDs concentrations (except 3.5 mg/mL) were observed in both cell lines. In the study conducted by Arkan et al. [51], the toxicity of CDs obtained from walnut oil using the hydrothermal method was measured by the MTT assay in 3 types of cells (HT29: human colon cancer cell line, PC3: prostatic small cell carcinoma, and MCF7: breast cancer cell line). The results showed that CDs were toxic at 1.25, 5, and 10 µg/mL, respectively. Similar to our study, potentially non-toxic CDs from citric acid were synthesized [52]. The results indicated that CDs at a concentration lower than 1 mg/mL have no toxicity on SKBR3 (breast cancer) and normal MCF-12A cell lines. Different in vivo models, e.g., zebrafish, rabbit, guinea pig, and rat, have been used to evaluate the toxicity of carbon-based nanomaterials and different results were reported depending on the growth stages of the animal models [53].

When oxidative stress specifically ROS is occurred, the resulting remarkable reactivity can severely damage proteins/ free amino acids, RNA/DNA, and lipids leading to cell death. According to Fig S6, TOS in TM4 cells increased with an increase in the concentration of CDs compared to the control group. In the same direction as the TM4 cytotoxic assay (Fig. 5), a significant increase was reported for CDs concentrations above 1.75 mg/mL, indicating cytotoxicity induction through oxidative stress. A concentration-dependent ROS production of zwitterionic CDs in HEK293 cells was reported [54]. In their work, high ROS levels were detected in the presence of CDs at 1.6 mg/mL concentration. Generally, CDs with positive zeta potential can generate high levels of ROS [55]. Many researchers assessed the toxicity of CDs in different cancer cell lines and reported that cells can tolerate considerably high CDs concentrations for extended periods without changing survival rates [56]. Further assessment focusing on the mechanism of action beyond the induced side effects by CDs on different cell lines, is warranted to clarify the possible signaling cascades for the observed CDs effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR and XRD analysis of CDs and BNC-CDs membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectroscopy was carried out on CDs, BNC, and BNC-CDs membrane to identify functional groups. As shown in Fig. 6 , in the CDs spectrum, the presence of the main characteristic bands at 3524 cm−1, 3392 cm−1 (stretching vibration of N-H or O-H bonds), 3031 cm−1 (bending vibrations of C-N groups), 2748 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of C–H), 1604 cm−1 (vibrations of C-O-C bonds), in the vicinity of 1450 cm−1 and 1246 cm−1 (stretching vibrations C-O and C–N) [57], with peaks at 1123 cm−1, 1044 cm−1, and 827 cm−1 (related to the vibration PO, P-O-C, and P-O-H, respectively) [58,59] can support the synthesis of heteroatoms CDs. This point should not be overlooked that the chemistries work reported elsewhere would support that various functional groups can generate a structure with different surface charges [60]. Therefore, the results and the characterized bands associated with hydroxyl, carboxylic, can be consistent with the negative charge of CDs [24]. In spectrum B, the characteristic peaks of BNC including the stretching vibration of OH and bending vibration C-O and C-C bonds of pyranoid rings can be observed at 3428 cm−1, and between 1200 and 1000 cm−1, respectively [61]. The band that appears at 1654 cm−1 has been assigned to the bending vibrations of H-O-H of the adsorbed water in BNC [62]. By comparing the FTIR spectrum of CDs and BNC and BNC-CDs (Fig. 6), it appears that the separate stretching vibration of the O-H and N-H bonds of CDs are not visible in the BNC-CDs which might indicate the inclusion of CDs in the BNC through these groups [62]. In addition, the observed shift related to the stretching vibrations of the C-O bond of CDs (1469 cm−1 and 1417 cm−1, spectrum A) to a lower wavenumber in the BNC-CDs (1406 cm−1, spectrum C) and the weakening of the C-N vibration (1246 cm−1, spectrum A) can confirm this statement [10].

The diffraction pattern of the hydrothermally synthesized CDs (Fig. S7C) represented a broad peak centered at 25.4°, which can be attributed to the diffraction of the graphite crystal plane (002) of CDs at 2θ:26.1° [39]. Therefore, based on evidence of the presence of N and P heteroatoms in CDs which was confirmed by XPS and FTIR, and in accordance with previous reports [37,39], the obtained pattern confirms the production of doped CDs. Also, the presented diffraction pattern of BNC (Fig. S7B) showed characteristic 2θ at 14.0° and 22.3° which can be indexed to the 1α and 1β phases of BNC structure. The 2θ: 15° can correspond to the 100α, 1101β or 0101β planes, while the sharp peak at 2θ: 22.5° can be referred to the 1101α or 2001β planes [63]. As illustrated in Fig. S6A, the XRD of the BNC-CDs showed two identical characteristic peaks of BNC at 2θ = 14.2° and 22.3°, however, the weak peak at 2θ: 22.5° could be attributed to the production of P-doped CDs [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and mechanical properties of BNC-CDs membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The FE-SEM micrographs confirm the nano-sized structure (<100 nm in diameter) of BNC with an intertwined fibrous and porous structure (Fig. S8A). In addition, the entrapment of CDs in the BNC structure can be observed (Fig. S8B). As expected, the addition of CDs to the BNC by the ex-situ method does not cause significant changes in the original arrangement and porous network and only fills the space between the fibrils and irregular pores (90–160 nm). This was confirmed by adding other functional compounds such as anthocyanins [62], MgO [64], and Lactobacillus sakei-derived bioactive metabolites [65] into the BNC.

The BNC is generally unique in its mechanical anisotropy, high YM, and fiber layer direction. It is noteworthy that the mechanical characteristics of BNC are influenced by a variety of factors, including the thickness of BNC, type of the BNC producing bacterial species, intermittent dehydration- rehydration, and the method of BNC modifications (i.e., in situ and ex situ) [22]. Also, the method of drying (oven or lyophilization) causes drastic changes in mechanical specifications. The effect of an additional substance on the reduction or improvement of the mechanical properties of the BNC is dependent on the method of incorporation, and the type and concentration of the substance [66]. In the current work, the addition of CDs into BNC altered the mechanical features (Table S1). The observation of an increase in the UTS and YM and a simultaneous decline in the STB of BNC-CDs membrane (Table S1), indicates the fact that the mechanical strength of the BNC membrane has diminished and the flexibility has increased after the addition of CDs. This is a condition that normally arises when an additional compound is added to the BNC by the ex situ method [10,24], which declines the intramolecular connections within the BNC. Mirtalebi et al. [64] showed that the addition of nanomaterials to the BNC through the in situ method did not significantly modify the STB of BNC. This is primarily due to the lower loading capacity of the in situ versus the ex situ method of BNC modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity of CDs and BNC-CDs membrane
ce:section.ce:para: CDs synthesized from natural carbon sources (e.g., plant and microbial biomass) by the hydrothermal technique are ideal for antimicrobial application [67]. The well-diffusion method was used to assess the antimicrobial activity of CDs. CDs showed antimicrobial activity on two Gram-positive bacteria, three Gram-negative bacteria, and four fungal strains, which occurred in a dose-dependent manner ( Table 1) ( Fig. 7).

According to Zanetti et al. [68], in the agar diffusion method, the antibacterial performance of nanomaterials can be categorized into four classes including non-sensitive (ZOI < 9 mm), sensitive (ZOI 9–14 mm), very sensitive (ZOI 14–17 mm), and extremely sensitive (ZOI > 17 mm). Therefore, based on the available data (Table 1), all investigated strains are extremely sensitive to CDs at a concentration of 100 mg/mL. No guideline exists to interpret the antifungal activity of nanomaterials. The antibacterial effect of an antimicrobial generally varies depending on factors such as the type of strain and the concentration of the agent [69]. Typically, positive-charged CDs simply interact with negatively charged microorganisms via electrostatic interactions, but in the case of CDs (zeta potential of −47.6 mV) synthesized from postbiotics of S. cerevisiae, van der Waals forces might overcome the repulsive forces between cells and CDs [70]. In current study, the zeta potential of CDs, L. monocytogenes, and E. coli are − 47.6, − 2.5, and − 4.95 mV [10], then, CDs can simply attach to L. monocytogenes than E. coli. The weak/moderate antimicrobial activity of current CDs could have been due to this fact that anionic and neutral CDs represent weaker antimicrobial performance than cationic CDs [31]. For example, Bing et al. [71] found that CDs with − 19.5, + 27.6 and 0.94 mV zeta potential exhibit different anti-E.coli activity. CDs with highly positive surface charge (+31 mV) with superior broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity on different bacterial strains was synthesized from pyrolysis of spermine and dopamine [6]. The variety in electrical characteristics of CDs from different sources indicates the differences that can be seen in the antimicrobial results of various works in which the performance of different CDs has been investigated on similar microbial strains. The microbial cell wall is considered a major factor in the susceptibility of a particular strain to an antimicrobial [72]. Although Gram-positive bacteria have thicker cell walls, Gram-negative bacteria have a thinner cell walls, but more complicated walls leading to high resistance to antimicrobials [10]. However, certain CDs interact with specific sites in the cell membrane of certain bacterial cells, whereas in other bacteria it does not have this ability [33]. A dose and concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity has been reported in CDs [73]. In our work, the most significant antimicrobial activity was observed in L. monocytogenes. Generally, CDs simply absorb to the loose membrane structure of Gram-positive bacteria, e.g., L. monocytogenes, and exhibit greater antibacterial activity than Gram-negative bacteria [74]. Muktha et al. [75] reported similar findings in which the relationship of increased CDs concentration to ZOI size was significant in all investigated species. It seems that our synthesized CDs have greater antimicrobial performance due to N and P-doping, which is occurring naturally during CDs synthesis from postbiotics of S. cerevisiae. According to Chatzimitakos and Stalikas [76], N-doping enhances the antimicrobial performance of CDs. Based on results reported by Roy et al. [46], S-doped turmeric CDs (8.5 nm; 5 mg/mL with a zeta potential of +4.2 mV) exhibited antibacterial activity (ZOI 14 mm) on E.coli, while non-functionalized turmeric CDs (10.6 nm with a zeta potential of −17.2 mV) had no antimicrobial performance, indicating the importance of CDs electrostatic properties and functionalization on their antimicrobial performance. In general, the antimicrobial effects of CDs are highly influenced by many factors related to CDs and microbial species. This can be one of the major drawbacks of using CDs as an antimicrobial compound.

The antifungal activity of CDs has also been reported. The results of our study suggested that all fungal strains were less sensitive to CDs than bacterial species (Table 1) (Fig. 7). This can be attributed to the interaction of CDs with eukaryotic fungal cells that are more complicated than bacterial cells. It is noteworthy that with the exceptions of A. flavus, the antifungal activity has been reinforced insignificantly by increasing CDs concentration. The highest size of ZOI was found in A. flavus at 100 mg/mL concentration of CDs. Jhonsi et al. [77] reported that CDs of tamarind at a concentration of 10 mg/mL has an antifungal effect on C. albicans. It seems that the starting material for CD synthesis, the method of synthesis, and CDs features can affect the antifungal activity of CDs on different microorganisms. For example, a size-dependent antifungal activity of CDs has been reported. CDs with small sizes exhibited higher antifungal activity [78]. Moreover, the method used to study the antimicrobial properties of CDs should be taken into account when comparing antimicrobial results from various literature, unfortunately, this is getting less consideration.

The 3D structure of BNC can be used to support antimicrobial agents, including chitosan [79], nisin [80], nanoparticles [81], and bioactive metabolites of lactic acid bacteria [65]. The antibacterial performance of the BNC-CDs membrane is depicted in Fig. S9. Similar to the well-diffusion method, the ZOI of BNC-CDs increased linearly against bacterial and fungal strains with an increase in CDs concentration. It is clear that Gram-positive bacteria are more sensitive to the BNC-CDs than Gram-negative and fungal strains, respectively. The greatest ZOI was found in L. monocytogenes. However, P. aeruginosa and A. flavus were found among strains susceptible to BNC-CDs membranes. Depending on the size of the ZOI, the antibacterial membrane can be classified into four groups, including non-sensitive, sensitive, very sensitive, and extremely sensitive films [20]. Therefore, a group of Gram-negative bacteria, E. coli, was sensitive (ZOI 9–14 mm) to BNC-CDs (22.33 mg/cm3), while L. monocytogenes, a Gram-positive bacterium, was extremely sensitive (ZOI > 20 mm). S. aureus, S. Typhimurium and P. aeruginosa were categorized as very sensitive (ZOI 15–19 mm) strains to the developed membrane. No criteria exist to interpret the antifungal activity of films. In a study, CDs of white mulberry were successfully incorporated into BNC membrane and the results showed that BNC-CDs in an optimized CDs concentration had ZOI of 10.45 mm on L. monocytogenes [24]. The extreme sensitivity of L. monocytogenes to the CDs and BNC-CDs antimicrobial membrane highlights the potential use of CDs and film on food or other surfaces that are naturally contaminated by this highly infectious pathogen. CDs have a small size and a large surface-to-volume ratio with the ability to destroy microorganisms by strong adherence behavior to the surface of the cell, generation of ROS, interfering with the cell membrane permeability, inducing oxidative stress, and conformational changes in intracellular biomolecules (e.g., DNA, RNA, proteins) [2,6,74,75]. Earlier studies have shown that CDs selectively target microbial cells and affect them more rapidly and significantly. This property can be linked to the more negative charge of the bacterial membrane than human cells. It is also related to the cell ability to set up an antioxidant system to remove excessive oxidative stress in humans [55]. For better understanding antimicrobial mechanism, further experiments including SEM, cell constituents' release, time-kill curve analysis, determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), etc. are needed.

The utilization of microbial biomass and by-products, which are rich in nitrogen, phosphor, and sulfur elements, lead to the synthesis of low toxic and heteroatom-doped C-dots, improving their practical application. In this study, non-toxic CDs with potent antimicrobial performance and extraordinary antioxidant activity have been successfully synthesized from postbiotics of S. cerevisiae. CDs had a dose-dependent antioxidant and antimicrobial activity on certain bacterial and fungal pathogens. CDs only at ≥ 7 mg/mL concentration were known to be cytotoxic in the human colon cancer cell line. As expected, the XPS and FTIR results supported the production of N and P-doped CDs, requiring further examination to identify the characteristics of this doping. CDs were successfully incorporated in BNC and exhibited a broad-spectrum antibacterial performance. The BNC-CDs film prepared in this study can be used in the preparation of antimicrobial/antioxidant films for food purposes. Moreover, due to the non-toxicity and antioxidant/antimicrobial performance, these CDs are recommended for use in treating animal and human infections or as food and feed additives.

Mahdi Ghorbani: Investigation and Writing – original draft. Hossein Tajik: Supervision and Visualization. Mehran Moradi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, and Writing – review & editing. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, and Writing – review & editing. Arash Alizadeh: Investigation and Writing – review & editing.
